splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuit courts disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability from multiemployer pension plans to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be selected by the statutory measurement date (end of prior plan year), providing employers with greater predictability about potential withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing plans to use more current data even if it means assumptions are chosen after year-end.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: Trustees of IAM National Pension Fund v. M & K Employee Solutions

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuit courts split on whether federal courts can apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee is a fugitive, allowing revocation proceedings even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release during absconding, and the term expires as set in the judgment regardless of the supervisee's fugitive status.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face the additional gatekeeping requirement of § 2244(b)(1) — which categorically bars raising any claim previously presented — when bringing successive collateral attacks under § 2255. If (b)(1) applies, it creates a stricter bar beyond § 2255(h)'s own requirements.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing an additional gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners under § 2255, meaning they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuit courts disagree on whether candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures like ballot-receipt deadlines. The outcome could reshape how courts evaluate candidate standing to challenge election rules nationwide.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate for federal office suffers a cognizable competitive injury sufficient to establish Article III standing when challenging a state election law that allegedly dilutes or undermines the integrity of votes.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that generalized concerns about election procedures are insufficient to confer Article III standing on a candidate absent a concrete, particularized injury.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to agency (or Sentencing Commission) interpretations when construing ambiguous provisions that increase criminal penalties or sentencing enhancements.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether courts should defer to agency or Sentencing Commission interpretations of ambiguous provisions that expand criminal liability, or whether the rule of lenity should prevail. This split has deepened after Loper Bright overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous provisions that bear on criminal liability or sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference applies even in the criminal context, allowing anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous sentencing provisions to control over the rule of lenity.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to be met for disqualification) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) means defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three listed criminal-history criteria or if meeting any single criterion is disqualifying. This directly affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses nationwide.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) disqualifies a defendant from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as 'or' in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) encompasses inchoate offenses like conspiracy when the guideline text does not mention them but Application Note 1 includes them.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes conspiracy and other inchoate offenses in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor. This affects career-offender sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior conspiracy convictions.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is controlling — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative interpretation of § 4B1.2(b) entitled to deference, and thus conspiracy and other inchoate offenses qualify as controlled substance offenses for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Application Note 1 exceeds guideline text — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses because the text does not mention them, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline's scope beyond its plain meaning.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law — particularly the First Step Act's elimination of mandatory consecutive 'stacked' sentences for § 924(c) offenses — as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically prohibit it while others allow it as one factor among many.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in sentencing law may contribute to or constitute extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release, either alone or in combination with other factors.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes categorically cannot support release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or in combination with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state criminalizes a substance not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in § 4B1.2(b) requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled, or whether any state-law drug offense qualifies regardless of federal scheduling. This affects career-offender and other sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: State offense qualifies regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' broadly includes any offense under state law, even if the substance is not federally controlled, because the text refers to 'an offense under . . . state law' without a federal-law limitation.

positions.label: Substance must be federally scheduled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the underlying substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 in good faith under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether property appreciation occurring between a Chapter 13 filing and a good-faith conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. This directly affects whether Chapter 7 trustees can sell homes that have appreciated to recover value for creditors.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with the broad scope of § 541(a), means that post-petition appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that § 348(f)(1)(A) was designed to protect good-faith converting debtors and that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners must use habeas corpus (28 U.S.C. § 2241) or may use civil rights actions (Bivens or other statutory causes of action) to challenge conditions of confinement or BOP decisions regarding the terms of imprisonment that do not directly challenge the fact or duration of confinement.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether challenges to conditions of confinement or BOP administrative decisions (such as First Step Act time-credit determinations) must be brought as habeas petitions or may proceed as civil rights claims. This affects federal prisoners' access to remedies and procedural requirements.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy for duration-related claims
positions.summary: Holds that challenges seeking speedier release or transfer to prerelease custody must be brought under habeas corpus statutes, not as civil rights actions, following Preiser's exclusivity rule.

positions.label: Civil rights claims may proceed for conditions challenges
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights claims to challenge conditions or terms of confinement that do not directly challenge the fact or duration of imprisonment, and habeas is not the exclusive remedy for such claims.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil appeal deadline (60 days under FRAP 4(a)) or the criminal appeal deadline (14 days under FRAP 4(b)) applies to appeals from orders denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is treated as a civil matter (subject to a 60-day appeal deadline) or a criminal matter (subject to a 14-day appeal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and the civil appeal deadline of 60 days under FRAP 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is part of the criminal case and the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under FRAP 4(b) governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may consider punitive purposes (punishment for the violation itself) when imposing sentences upon revocation of supervised release, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to the purposes of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts revoking supervised release may expressly impose sentences to punish the defendant for the violating conduct, or whether § 3583(e) limits revocation sentences to the breach-of-trust rationale. The Sixth Circuit allows express punishment while other circuits restrict it.

positions.label: Punishment for violation is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly consider punishment for the supervised release violation itself as a purpose of the revocation sentence.

positions.label: Punishment is not a permissible purpose
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation sentences should reflect the breach of the defendant's trust and the need for appropriate sanctions, but not punish the defendant for the underlying violation conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the context of hospitality or public accommodation settings, including the scope of available remedies.
splits.description: The Second Circuit's approach to discrimination claims in hospitality settings is under SCOTUS review, suggesting a split among circuits on the standards for proving and remedying discrimination claims against business establishments under federal civil rights statutes.

positions.label: Broader liability standards
positions.summary: Holds that discrimination claims against hospitality businesses should be evaluated under standards that more readily permit plaintiffs to establish liability and recover damages.

positions.label: More restrictive standards
positions.summary: Holds that discrimination claims against business operators require more rigorous proof or apply narrower standards for liability and available remedies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prevents states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any . . . broker,' preempts common-law negligence claims against freight brokers arising from carrier-caused accidents. This affects liability allocation in the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted by the FAAAA because broker services in arranging transportation are 'related to' a service of a broker.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that traditional state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they do not regulate broker prices, routes, or services in the relevant sense.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that could also be raised in habeas proceedings under § 2255 — such as allegations of legal error in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered as extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits allow it while others hold § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used to circumvent § 2255.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claims of legal error in the underlying conviction or sentence as one factor in the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider — must use § 2255
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used to challenge the validity of a conviction or sentence; such claims must be raised through § 2255 habeas proceedings.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the scope of the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'engaged in . . . interstate commerce.' This affects the enforceability of arbitration agreements for a wide range of distribution workers.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are part of a continuous interstate transportation chain, including those making last-mile deliveries of goods that have moved in interstate commerce, qualify as transportation workers exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that distribution workers delivering goods locally to retail stores are not 'transportation workers engaged in . . . interstate commerce' under Section 1 because they are not themselves moving goods across state lines.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state-law failure-to-warn claims against pesticide manufacturers when the claims are based on labeling that the EPA approved. This affects product liability litigation involving agricultural chemicals and consumer pesticides nationwide.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling different from or in addition to what the EPA has approved.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims because they impose requirements 'parallel to' rather than 'in addition to' FIFRA's labeling requirements, consistent with the Supreme Court's Bates v. Dow Agrosciences framework.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in New York State Rifle & Pistol Ass'n v. Bruen, the circuits have divided on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by marijuana users is consistent with the Second Amendment's text and historical tradition. This is increasingly significant as more states legalize marijuana.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals who use intoxicating substances.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical analogue justifying the disarmament of persons who use marijuana.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
